---
title: "Module 02: Regularization — L1, L2, Lasso, Ridge, and Elastic Net"
---

## Learning objectives

- Explain **why** we penalize coefficients when predictors are correlated or \(p\) is large relative to \(n\).
- Contrast the **\(L_2\)** penalty (ridge) and **\(L_1\)** penalty (lasso) in words and with a simple geometry picture.
- Describe **elastic net** as a blend controlled by **`alpha`** in `glmnet`.
- Compare **continuous shrinkage** (penalized regression) with **discrete** subset search (AIC / stepwise).
- Link each idea to the **Day 1 (Monday) slides** and the **gene / penguin** notebooks.

[← Module library](index.qmd) · [Module 01 — Foundations](module-01-foundations.qmd) · [Module 04 — Pipeline](module-04-pipeline.qmd)

---

## On the slides (Day 1)

| Topic | Where in the deck |
|-------|-------------------|
| Gene × trait: null, full, AIC, lasso | [Part C — Gene trait](../slides/day-01-monday.html#/part-c-gene-trait) |
| Lasso motivation | [Lasso vs discrete search](../slides/day-01-monday.html#/lasso-regression) |
| Cross-validation (LOO and \(k\)-fold) | [CV concepts](../slides/day-01-monday.html#/cross-validation) · [LOO](../slides/day-01-monday.html#/loo-cv) · [\(k\)-fold](../slides/day-01-monday.html#/k-fold-cv) |
| Cross-validated \(\lambda\) | [Lasso CV](../slides/day-01-monday.html#/lasso-cv) |
| Logistic lasso | [Part D — Gene disease](../slides/day-01-monday.html#/part-d-gene-disease) |
| Elastic net | [Elastic net slide](../slides/day-01-monday.html#/elastic-net) |
| Correlated predictors (Hald) | [Day 1 deck](../slides/day-01-monday.qmd) — Hald cement block |

*Edit slides in* [`day-01-monday.qmd`](../slides/day-01-monday.qmd)*; links above target the **rendered** Reveal deck.*

---

## Why penalize?

Ordinary least squares (OLS) or full logistic regression minimizes **only** prediction error on the training rows. That is fine when:

- \(n\) is comfortably larger than the number of effective parameters, and  
- predictors are not so correlated that coefficient signs flip when you add or drop one column.

When those conditions fail — as in the **Hald cement** example on Monday or with **many gene columns** — you often see:

- **Inflated variance** of individual \(\hat\beta_j\) (wide confidence intervals).  
- **Unstable subsets** under stepwise search (AIC picks different models on nearby samples).  
- **Overfitting** on training data (great in-sample fit, weaker on new rows).

**Regularization** adds a penalty on the size (or sparsity) of coefficients. You accept a controlled amount of **bias** to gain **lower variance** and more stable predictions on new data. See [Module 01](module-01-foundations.qmd) for train / validation / test discipline when choosing penalty strength.

---

## The \(L_2\) norm and ridge regression

### Penalty

Ridge minimizes (for linear regression):

\[
\sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_i - \hat y_i)^2 + \lambda \sum_{j=1}^{p} \beta_j^2
\]

The second term is the **squared Euclidean norm** of the coefficient vector — an **\(L_2\)** penalty. \(\lambda \ge 0\) controls strength: \(\lambda = 0\) is OLS; larger \(\lambda\) shrinks all \(\beta_j\) toward zero.

### Geometry (intuition)

Think of OLS as finding the best fit inside the usual least-squares “bowl.” Ridge restricts coefficients to lie inside a **sphere** (round constraint in \(\beta\)-space). The fit is where the bowl touches that sphere — typically **all** coefficients are non-zero but **smaller** than OLS.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
  OLS[OLS: any beta]
  Ridge[Ridge: L2 ball around 0]
  OLS --> Ridge
```

### When ridge helps

- Many predictors move together (multicollinearity) — ridge **stabilizes** their joint effect instead of exploding one coefficient.  
- You want **shrinkage** but do **not** need exact zeros for interpretation.

### In R (`glmnet`)

Ridge is **`alpha = 0`** in `glmnet` (see minimal example below).

---

## The \(L_1\) norm and lasso

### Penalty

Lasso uses:

\[
\sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_i - \hat y_i)^2 + \lambda \sum_{j=1}^{p} |\beta_j|
\]

The penalty is the **\(L_1\)** norm (sum of absolute values). That changes the geometry.

### Geometry (intuition)

The constraint region is a **diamond** (octahedron in higher dimensions) with **corners on the axes**. The least-squares surface often first touches a **corner**, where some \(\beta_j = 0\) **exactly** — automatic **variable selection** plus prediction.

### When lasso helps

- You believe **only a subset** of predictors matter (gene simulation on Monday).  
- You want a **sparse** model for communication or follow-up experiments.

### Tradeoffs

- If a group of correlated predictors all matter, lasso may pick **one** and zero the rest (arbitrary within the group).  
- **Elastic net** (below) partly fixes that.

### In R (`glmnet`)

Lasso is **`alpha = 1`**.

---

## Ridge vs lasso vs OLS (summary)

| Method | Penalty | Coefficients | Variable selection | Correlated \(x\) |
|--------|---------|--------------|--------------------|------------------|
| OLS | none | unbiased-ish | no | unstable |
| Ridge | \(L_2\) | shrunk, rarely 0 | no | stabilized |
| Lasso | \(L_1\) | sparse | yes | can be arbitrary |
| Elastic net | mix | sparse + grouped shrink | partial | compromise |

---

## AIC and stepwise (discrete alternative)

**AIC stepwise** searches over **subsets** of predictors (which columns are in / out). Each step is **discrete** — a different model class, not a smooth path in \(\beta\).

| Aspect | Stepwise / AIC | Lasso |
|--------|----------------|-------|
| Search | combinatorial subsets | continuous shrinkage path in \(\lambda\) |
| Stability | can jump between models | path is smoother in \(\lambda\) |
| Correlated \(x\) | may pick one arbitrarily | similar issue at \(\lambda\) values that force sparsity |

Monday compares **backward AIC** and **lasso** coefficient panels on the **same gene data** — good classroom contrast ([lasso slide](../slides/day-01-monday.html#/lasso-regression)).

---

## Elastic net

**Elastic net** blends \(L_1\) and \(L_2\):

\[
\text{loss} + \lambda \left[ (1-\alpha)\frac{1}{2}\sum_j \beta_j^2 + \alpha \sum_j |\beta_j| \right]
\]

In **`glmnet`**, **`alpha`** \(\in [0,1]\) is the mixing parameter:

| `alpha` | Effect |
|---------|--------|
| `0` | Ridge only |
| `1` | Lasso only |
| between | some grouping/shrinkage (ridge-like) **and** some exact zeros (lasso-like) |

Use elastic net when predictors are **correlated** but you still want **some sparsity** — a common “unsure” default in high-dimensional tabular work.

**Slides:** [Elastic net (Monday)](../slides/day-01-monday.html#/elastic-net)

---

## Choosing \(\lambda\) (penalty strength)

- **`lambda`** controls how hard you shrink. Too small → like OLS; too large → all coefficients near zero.  
- **`cv.glmnet`** (or `tidymodels` tuning) estimates error on **held-out folds** while varying \(\lambda\).  
- **`lambda.1se`** rule: pick the **largest** \(\lambda\) whose CV error is within one standard error of the minimum — **simpler** model, still near-optimal CV loss (Monday gene trait and disease blocks).

**Honesty:** paths and CV curves in class often use **all** rows for the plot; for a **final** performance claim, lock a test set or nest tuning inside training only ([Module 01](module-01-foundations.qmd)).

---

## Minimal R: ridge and lasso on penguins

Same predictors as the pipeline modules: Adelie vs Gentoo, bill + island + sex + year. **`scale()`** predictors before `glmnet` (penalties are not invariant to units).

```{r}
#| label: m02-glmnet-demo
#| echo: true
#| message: false
#| warning: false
library(glmnet)
library(palmerpenguins)
library(dplyr)

peng <- palmerpenguins::penguins |>
  filter(species %in% c("Adelie", "Gentoo")) |>
  mutate(y = as.integer(species == "Gentoo")) |>
  select(bill_length_mm, bill_depth_mm, island, sex, year, y) |>
  tidyr::drop_na()

x <- model.matrix(y ~ bill_length_mm + bill_depth_mm + island + sex + year, peng)[, -1]
y <- peng$y
x_s <- scale(x)

cv_ridge <- cv.glmnet(x_s, y, family = "binomial", alpha = 0, nfolds = 5)
cv_lasso <- cv.glmnet(x_s, y, family = "binomial", alpha = 1, nfolds = 5)

data.frame(
  model = c("Ridge (alpha=0)", "Lasso (alpha=1)"),
  lambda_1se = c(cv_ridge$lambda.1se, cv_lasso$lambda.1se),
  n_nonzero = c(
    sum(coef(cv_ridge, s = "lambda.1se") != 0) - 1L,
    sum(coef(cv_lasso, s = "lambda.1se") != 0) - 1L
  )
)
```

Try **`alpha = 0.5`** for elastic net on the same `x_s`, `y`. For **continuous** body mass, see [penguins-body-mass.Rmd](../notebooks/penguins-body-mass.Rmd).

---

## Course links

| Resource | Role |
|----------|------|
| [Day 1 slides](../slides/day-01-monday.qmd) | Hald, gene trait/disease, lasso paths, elastic net |
| [Linear regression + LASSO (genes)](../notebooks/linear-regression-lasso.Rmd) | Full trait story with `MASS::stepAIC`, `glmnet` |
| [Logistic + tree (genes)](../notebooks/logistic-regression-gene-disease.Rmd) | Logistic lasso + `rpart` |
| [Penguins body mass](../notebooks/penguins-body-mass.Rmd) | Ridge/lasso on real measurements |
| [Curriculum outline](../curriculum-outline.qmd) | Day 1 regularization sequence |
| [Module 01 — Foundations](module-01-foundations.qmd) | Splits and generalization |
| [Module 04 — Pipeline](module-04-pipeline.qmd) | Same penguin table with `tidymodels` trees |

### Optional reading (instructors)

Post-selection inference after lasso is subtle; one sentence for curious students: *inference on “selected” coefficients generally needs specialized tools — do not treat lasso zeros as a free hypothesis test.*
